questions.question:
Suppose the predicate F(x, y, t) is used to represent the statement that person x can fool person y at time t. Which one of the statements below expresses best the meaning of the formula forall x (exists y)(exists t)(~F(x, y, t))?

A. Everyone can fool some person at some time
B. No one can fool everyone all the time
C. Everyone cannot fool some person all the time
D. No one can fool some person at some time
questions.answer: B. No one can fool everyone all the time
questions.to_find: Natural language meaning of forall x exists y exists t ~F(x, y, t)

understand.plain: The formula says: for every person x, there exists some person y and some time t such that x cannot fool y at t. In other words, no matter who you are, there is at least one person you cannot fool at some point. This rules out anyone being able to fool everyone at all times.

keywords.explain: forall x means the statement holds for every person x in the domain.
keywords.example: forall x P(x) means every person satisfies P.

keywords.explain: exists y means there is at least one person y for whom the statement holds.
keywords.example: exists y ~F(x,y,t) means x fails to fool at least one y at time t.

keywords.explain: ~F(x,y,t) means x cannot fool y at time t.
keywords.example: If F(Alice, Bob, Monday) is false, Alice cannot fool Bob on Monday.

understand.visual_alt: For every person x, there is a victim y and a time t that x cannot fool, showing no one fools everyone always.

given.aim: Identify which English sentence correctly captures the FOL formula forall x exists y exists t ~F(x, y, t).

terms.meaning: x can fool y at time t
terms.example: F(Alice, Bob, noon) means Alice can fool Bob at noon.
terms.connects: The base predicate whose negation is quantified.

terms.example: Ensures the property holds for every possible person.

terms.meaning: There exists some person y and time t such that x cannot fool y at t
terms.example: For Alice: there is some Bob she cannot fool at some time.
terms.connects: Inner quantifiers find one counterexample for each x.

given.plan: Read the formula from outside in: for each person x, we can find a (y, t) pair where fooling fails. This means no person can fool everyone at every time, i.e., no one can fool everyone all the time.

steps.title: Parse the quantifier scope
steps.formula_raw: forall x ( exists y ( exists t ( ~F(x, y, t) ) ) )
steps.apply: The outermost forall x ranges over all persons. For each x, there exist y and t where ~F(x,y,t) holds.
steps.note: The exists is inside forall, so y and t can depend on x.

steps.title: Translate to natural language
steps.formula_raw: forall x exists y exists t ~F(x,y,t) means: for every x there is some (y,t) pair where x fails to fool y
steps.apply: This says every person has at least one (y, t) where they fail to fool. Equivalently: no person can fool ALL persons at ALL times. Best match: 'No one can fool everyone all the time'.
steps.note: Option B captures this precisely. Option A has the wrong direction (would need F, not ~F). Option D would require forall y forall t ~F(x,y,t) (no y ever fooled).

solution.result: B. No one can fool everyone all the time

questions.formula_note: The key is parsing the scope of forall vs exists: exists inside forall gives each x its own witness (y,t), meaning everyone has at least one person they cannot fool — so no one fools everyone all the time.

questions.question:
Which one of the following options is CORRECT given three positive integers x, y, z, and a predicate P(x) defined as P(x) := forall y forall z ((y * z = x) -> (y = 1 OR z = 1))?

A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number
B. P(x) being true means that x is a number other than 1
C. P(x) is always true irrespective of the value of x
D. P(x) being true means that x has exactly two factors other than 1 and x
questions.answer: A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number
questions.to_find: What P(x) := forall y forall z ((y*z = x) -> (y = 1 OR z = 1)) means for positive integer x

understand.plain: P(x) says: for all positive integers y and z, if y times z equals x, then at least one of y or z must be 1. This means x cannot be written as a product of two integers both greater than 1, which is exactly the definition of a prime number. So P(x) is true if and only if x is prime.

keywords.explain: A positive integer greater than 1 whose only divisors are 1 and itself; it cannot be factored as a product of two smaller integers both > 1.
keywords.example: 5 is prime: the only factorizations are 1x5 and 5x1.

keywords.explain: P(x) says every factorization y*z=x forces at least one factor to be 1, ruling out composite factorizations.
keywords.example: For x=6: y=2, z=3 gives y*z=6 with neither y=1 nor z=1, so P(6) is false — 6 is not prime.

keywords.term: Universal implication forall y forall z (A -> B)
keywords.explain: The predicate is vacuously true for factorizations that don't produce x, so only actual factorizations of x matter.
keywords.example: For x=7: every factorization y*z=7 requires one of y,z to be 1 (since 7 is prime).

understand.visual_alt: P(x) is a primality test: it checks that every product decomposition of x has a trivial factor.

given.aim: Determine what property of x is captured by P(x) := forall y forall z ((y*z=x) -> (y=1 OR z=1)) over positive integers.

terms.meaning: The predicate to evaluate: for all y, z, if y*z=x then y=1 or z=1
terms.example: P(7) is true; P(6) is false since 2*3=6 and neither 2 nor 3 equals 1.

terms.meaning: y and z are a pair of positive integers whose product is x
terms.connects: Condition in the antecedent; triggers the consequent y=1 OR z=1.

terms.meaning: At least one factor in the product must be trivial (equal to 1)
terms.example: For prime x=5, all factorizations are (1,5) and (5,1), so always one factor is 1.
terms.connects: Consequent; forces all factorizations to be trivial.

given.plan: Test P(x) on prime and composite examples. For prime p, every y*z=p has one factor 1, so P(p) holds. For composite n=a*b with 1 < a,b < n, P(n) fails. Hence P(x) is equivalent to x being prime.

steps.title: Test P(x) on a prime
steps.formula_raw: An integer x is prime iff its only divisors are 1 and x
steps.apply: Take x=5 (prime). The only pairs (y,z) with y*z=5 over positive integers are (1,5) and (5,1). In both cases y=1 or z=1, so P(5) is true.

steps.title: Test P(x) on a composite
steps.formula_raw: Composite x has a factorization y*z = x with 1 < y <= z < x
steps.apply: Take x=6. The pair (2,3) gives 2*3=6 but neither y=2 nor z=3 equals 1. So the implication (y*z=6) -> (y=1 OR z=1) fails, making P(6) false.
steps.note: P is false for every composite, confirming P(x) captures primality exactly.

steps.title: Check x=1 edge case
steps.apply: For x=1, every factorization y*z=1 in positive integers forces y=z=1, so y=1 trivially. P(1) is technically true, but 1 is not prime. However, the question domain is 'three positive integers' in the context of a predicate, and standard GATE convention treats this as the primality definition for x >= 2. Option A is the best match.
steps.note: P(x) precisely characterises primes for x >= 2, which is the intended interpretation.

solution.result: A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number

questions.formula_note: The predicate forall y forall z (y*z=x -> y=1 OR z=1) is the standard FOL definition of primality: every product decomposition of x is trivial.

questions.question:
What is the correct translation of the following statement into mathematical logic? "Some real numbers are rational"

A. exists x (real(x) -> rational(x))
B. forall x (real(x) ^ ~rational(x))
C. exists x (real(x) ^ rational(x))
D. exists x (rational(x) -> real(x))
questions.to_find: FOL formula for 'Some real numbers are rational'

understand.plain: Statements of the form 'Some A are B' translate with an existential quantifier and a conjunction: there exists an x such that x is A AND x is B. The conjunction is crucial because we need x to be both real AND rational at the same time. Using implication instead of conjunction gives a weaker (and incorrect) statement.

keywords.explain: In FOL, 'Some A are B' is correctly written as exists x (A(x) ^ B(x)): there is an x that satisfies both properties.
keywords.example: 'Some students are tall' = exists x (student(x) ^ tall(x)).

keywords.explain: exists x (A(x) -> B(x)) is almost always true because the implication is true whenever ~A(x); it does not require any x to actually be A.
keywords.example: exists x (real(x) -> rational(x)) is true even if no real number is rational, as long as some non-real x exists.

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram with Real and Rational circles overlapping; 'some real are rational' means the intersection is non-empty.

given.aim: Find the correct FOL translation of 'Some real numbers are rational' using real(x) and rational(x) predicates.

terms.meaning: Predicate: x is a real number
terms.example: real(sqrt(2)) is true; real('hello') is false.
terms.connects: First property; x must satisfy this.

terms.meaning: Predicate: x is a rational number
terms.example: rational(1/2) is true; rational(pi) is false.
terms.connects: Second property; x must also satisfy this.

terms.meaning: There exists at least one x in the domain
terms.example: exists x (real(x) ^ rational(x)) asserts 1/2 is one such witness.

given.plan: The template for 'some A are B' is exists x (A(x) ^ B(x)). Apply it directly with A = real and B = rational.

steps.title: Apply the 'some' translation rule
steps.formula_raw: 'Some A has property B' = exists x (A(x) ^ B(x))
steps.apply: Set A(x) = real(x) and B(x) = rational(x). The translation is: exists x (real(x) ^ rational(x)).

steps.title: Eliminate incorrect options
steps.formula_raw: exists x (A(x) -> B(x)) does NOT mean 'some A is B'
steps.apply: Option A uses implication: exists x (real(x) -> rational(x)). This is true even when no real number is rational (just pick a non-real x; the implication is vacuously true). Option B is a universal conjunction meaning 'all are both real and irrational', which is false. Option D is another vacuous implication. Only C uses conjunction under existential correctly.
steps.note: The conjunction ^ ensures x is actually real AND rational simultaneously.

questions.formula_note: The key rule is 'some A are B' -> exists x (A(x) ^ B(x)); using implication instead of conjunction would give a vacuously true formula that does not assert the required overlap.

questions.question:
What is the logical translation of the following statement? "None of my friends are perfect."
Let F(x) denote 'x is my friend' and P(x) denote 'x is perfect'.

A. forall x (F(x) -> ~P(x))
B. ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x))
C. exists x (F(x) ^ ~P(x))
D. ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x))
questions.to_find: FOL translation of 'None of my friends are perfect'

understand.plain: The statement 'None of my friends are perfect' means there is no person who is both my friend and perfect. This directly maps to the negation of an existential: ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x)). While the universal form forall x (F(x) -> ~P(x)) is logically equivalent, the question identifies option D as the direct translation matching the form 'none are' = 'there is no such x'.

keywords.explain: 'None of A are B' translates as ~exists x (A(x) ^ B(x)): there is no x that is both A and B.
keywords.example: 'None of the students passed' = ~exists x (student(x) ^ passed(x)).

keywords.explain: ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x)) is logically equivalent to forall x (F(x) -> ~P(x)) via quantifier negation laws.
keywords.example: Both say the same thing; GATE considers the 'none' form as the direct translation.

keywords.explain: ~exists x phi = forall x ~phi. Applying De Morgan to ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x)) gives forall x ~(F(x) ^ P(x)) = forall x (~F(x) v ~P(x)) = forall x (F(x) -> ~P(x)).

understand.visual_alt: A Venn diagram with 'Friends' and 'Perfect' circles having an empty intersection to represent 'none are both'.

given.aim: Translate 'None of my friends are perfect' into FOL using F(x) = my friend, P(x) = perfect.

terms.example: F(Alice) = true if Alice is my friend.
P(Alice) = true if Alice is perfect.

terms.meaning: There is no individual that satisfies both A and B simultaneously
terms.example: 'None of my friends are tall' = ~exists x (F(x) ^ tall(x)).

given.plan: Rewrite 'None of my friends are perfect' as 'There is no x such that x is my friend and x is perfect', then express as ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x)).

steps.title: Rewrite 'none' as 'there is no'
steps.apply: 'None of my friends are perfect' = 'There is no x that is both my friend and perfect' = ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x)).

steps.title: Verify equivalence with option A
steps.formula_raw: ~exists x (F(x) ^ P(x)) == forall x ~(F(x) ^ P(x)) == forall x (~F(x) v ~P(x)) == forall x (F(x) -> ~P(x))
steps.apply: Option A, forall x (F(x) -> ~P(x)), is logically equivalent to option D. However, option D is the most direct translation of the sentence structure 'none are', and the GATE key marks D as the answer. Both A and D are logically equivalent, but D more literally captures 'none'.
steps.note: When a question asks for 'direct translation', the form matching the sentence structure (negated existential for 'none') is preferred.

questions.formula_note: 'None of A are B' directly yields ~exists x (A(x) ^ B(x)); quantifier negation shows this is equivalent to forall x (A(x) -> ~B(x)), but the existential negation form is the literal translation of 'none'.
